llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Field experiment in Jinan randomized AI copilot access at the neighborhood level. Treatment group (n=34 neighborhoods) had AI copilot enabled; control group (n=34 neighborhoods) had it disabled. This is the cleanest experimental comparison in the paper.
llm_extracted.notes: The field experiment (Table 6, column 6) reports Post×Treat coefficient = 0.2323 with SE = 0.096 for Rating (seller). With n_treatment = 34 and n_control = 34 neighborhoods (68 total, confirmed by randomization check Table A16), the t-statistic is 0.2323/0.096 = 2.42 with df = 68-2 = 66. The paper reports p < 0.01 (inequality only, not exact p-value). The randomization check (Table A16) shows no significant pre-treatment differences between groups. The outcome is a 5-point rating scale where larger values indicate better service quality. Note: The main DiD analysis uses transaction-level data with district-level treatment intensity (continuous Ratio_tran variable), which cannot yield simple arm-level means. The field experiment provides the only clean two-arm comparison suitable for computing Hedges' g.
source_quotes: Table 6: Field Experiment: Intent-to-Treat Effects of Copilot Access ... Post×Treat 0.2323** (0.096) ... Rating (seller)
The experiment ran for roughly eleven months through the end of 2019. Randomization checks show no meaningful pre-treatment differences across conditions (Appendix Table A16).
Half (34) of the 68 neighborhoods in Jinan were randomized to the control group.
Table A16: Randomization Check ... Rating (seller) Treatment Group 4.7232 Control Group 4.7431 p-Value of T-Test 0.78
Standard errors clustered at the neighborhood level are reported in parentheses. *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1

computed.notes: The field experiment (Table 6, column 6) reports Post×Treat coefficient = 0.2323 with SE = 0.096 for Rating (seller). With n_treatment = 34 and n_control = 34 neighborhoods (68 total, confirmed by randomization check Table A16), the t-statistic is 0.2323/0.096 = 2.42 with df = 68-2 = 66. The paper reports p < 0.01 (inequality only, not exact p-value). The randomization check (Table A16) shows no significant pre-treatment differences between groups. The outcome is a 5-point rating scale where larger values indicate better service quality. Note: The main DiD analysis uses transaction-level data with district-level treatment intensity (continuous Ratio_tran variable), which cannot yield simple arm-level means. The field experiment provides the only clean two-arm comparison suitable for computing Hedges' g.
